mechanism_narrative: Consistent quarterly GDP growth (GDPC1) signals expanding economic activity and rising corporate earnings, which drives S&P 500 prices higher over the subsequent quarter. This is a persistent trend-following signal that is always active.
evidence_refs: fact:Historical correlation between GDP growth and equity returns
failure_condition: If the rolling 2-year correlation between GDPC1 quarterly change and S&P 500 forward 60-day returns is less than 0.2, or if the signal produces negative average returns in walk-forward testing over any 3-year window.

mechanism_narrative: A rise in the unemployment rate (UNRATE) above 6% indicates labor market weakness and reduced consumer spending, which negatively impacts corporate profits and leads to declining S&P 500 returns over the next month.
failure_condition: If in triggered instances (UNRATE > 6.0%), S&P 500 forward 20-day returns are positive more than 50% of the time, or if the average return is not significantly negative (t-test p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate is extremely low (near zero) and retail sales (RSAFS) are growing month-over-month, the combination signals that monetary accommodation is still supporting demand without inflationary pressure, boosting equity valuations in the short term.
regime_hint: zero lower bound with moderate demand growth
failure_condition: If the joint signal (FEDFUNDS < 0.25% and RSAFS growth > 0.5%) shows a median forward 20-day return at or below zero across all historical episodes, or if its win rate is less than 55%.

mechanism_narrative: A decline in industrial production (INDPRO) below the index level of 100 signals a contraction in manufacturing activity, which precedes broader economic slowdown and negative S&P 500 returns over the next quarter.
failure_condition: If after INDPRO falls below 100, the average S&P 500 forward 60-day return is not negative, or if the strategy fails to outperform a buy-and-hold by at least 2% annually in simulated walk-forward.

mechanism_narrative: Persistent inflation as measured by CPI (CPIAUCSL) erodes purchasing power and prompts tightening expectations, which compress equity valuations and lead to negative S&P 500 returns over a one-month horizon.
evidence_refs: fact:Inflation negatively correlated with forward returns in moderate regimes
failure_condition: If the correlation between CPIAUCSL monthly change and forward 20-day returns is consistently positive over rolling 3-year windows, or if a long-short portfolio based on CPI momentum produces negative Sharpe ratio over 5 years.

mechanism_narrative: Rising nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) combined with increasing housing starts (HOUST) provides a powerful composite signal of robust labor market and construction activity, driving S&P 500 returns higher over the subsequent week as the market prices in strong near-term demand.
failure_condition: If the joint signal (both PAYEMS > 140k and HOUST > 1000) shows a negative average forward 5-day return, or if its win rate is below 50% in out-of-sample testing.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp drop in retail sales (RSAFS) below 430,000 million dollars signals weakening consumer demand, which directly reduces corporate revenue expectations and leads to negative S&P 500 returns over the next month.
failure_condition: If in triggered instances (RSAFS < 430000), the S&P 500 forward 20-day return is not significantly negative (mean > -0.5% or p-value > 0.05), or if the signal fires less than 5 times in the test period.

mechanism_narrative: In the zero lower bound regime (FEDFUNDS below 0.25%), the market responds positively to any improvement in economic data because it reduces recession risk without triggering tightening fears. This hypothesis posits that rising retail sales (RSAFS) should lead to positive returns in such an environment.
evidence_refs: fact:Zero-bound equity response to positive data
failure_condition: If in the zero lower bound regime (FEDFUNDS < 0.25%), the forward 5-day returns following positive RSAFS months are not significantly positive (mean < 0.5% or win rate < 55%), or if the strategy has negative cumulative returns over any consecutive 2-year window.
